content.body:
Electric field:
E = F/q (force per unit charge)
For point charge: E = kQ/r² (k=9×10⁹ N⋅m²/C²)
Field lines: from positive → negative

Capacitor:
C = Q/V (capacitance = charge/voltage)
Unit: Farad (F)
Energy stored: E = ½CV² = Q²/2C = ½QV

Series: 1/C_total = 1/C₁ + 1/C₂
Parallel: C_total = C₁ + C₂

content.title: Capacitor Calculations
content.body:
C=100μF, V=12V
Q=CV=100×10⁻⁶×12=1.2×10⁻³ C
E=½CV²=½×100×10⁻⁶×144=7.2×10⁻³ J

Two 6μF capacitors in series:
1/C=1/6+1/6=2/6, C=3μF
In parallel: C=6+6=12μF

content.title: Electric Fields
content.body:
Uniform field between parallel plates:
E = V/d (V=voltage, d=separation)

Field lines always perpendicular to equipotentials
Closer lines = stronger field
Parallel plates: uniform field between them

questions.question: Electric field strength E =
questions.explanation: E = force per unit charge = F/q, units N/C or V/m

questions.question: Capacitance C =
questions.explanation: C = Q/V. Larger capacitance: stores more charge per volt

questions.question: 100μF capacitor at 10V. Charge stored =
Energy in capacitor = ½CV². C=50μF, V=20V. Energy =
Two 4μF capacitors in series. Total capacitance =
Two 4μF capacitors in parallel. Total capacitance =
questions.explanation: Parallel: C=4+4=8μF

questions.question: Electric field lines go from
questions.explanation: Field lines run from positive to negative charges, showing direction of force on positive test charge

questions.question: Between parallel plates (V=100V, d=0.01m). Field E =
Capacitors in parallel all have the same
questions.explanation: Parallel: same voltage across each capacitor (connected across same two nodes)

questions.question: What is a Farad?
questions.explanation: 1 Farad = 1 C/V (one coulomb stored per volt)

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Mains electricity:
Voltage: 240V (rms) at 50Hz in Uganda/UK
Three-pin plug wires:
Live (brown/red): carries alternating voltage
Neutral (blue/black): return path, near 0V
Earth (green-yellow): safety connection

Fuses: melt if current too high (thin wire)
Circuit breakers: electromagnetic switch trips
RCDs: detect current imbalance → trip instantly

Power: P=IV=I²R=V²/R
Cost: units(kWh) = power(kW) × time(h)

content.title: Electrical Safety
content.body:
Fuse rating: slightly above normal operating current
Toaster: P=1500W, V=240V: I=P/V=6.25A → use 10A fuse
Double insulation: no earth needed (class II appliance)
Earth wire: connects metal casing to ground → fault current flows safely instead of through user

content.title: Electricity Cost
content.body:
Kettle: 2kW used for 15 min = 0.5h
Units = 2×0.5 = 1 kWh
Cost at UGX 500/kWh = UGX 500

Refrigerator: 0.1kW for 24h
Units = 0.1×24 = 2.4 kWh/day = 72 kWh/month

questions.question: In a 3-pin plug, the live wire colour is
questions.explanation: Live wire (brown in UK, red in older): carries the alternating high voltage

questions.question: The earth wire protects by
options: connecting metal casing to ground so fault current flows safely
questions.answer: connecting metal casing to ground so fault current flows safely
questions.explanation: If live wire touches metal casing, earth wire provides low-resistance path for fault current → trips fuse/breaker

questions.question: A fuse with correct rating should
options: blow only when current exceeds safe level
blow at exactly rated current always
questions.answer: blow only when current exceeds safe level
questions.explanation: Fuse melts at slightly above normal current, protecting circuit from overload or short circuit

questions.question: 1500W kettle on 240V. Current drawn =
Correct fuse for 6.25A appliance from options
questions.explanation: Choose fuse rated slightly ABOVE normal current: 10A is next standard size above 6.25A

questions.question: kWh means
options: energy used by 1kW device in 1 hour
questions.answer: energy used by 1kW device in 1 hour
questions.explanation: 1 kWh = 1 kilowatt × 1 hour = 3,600,000 J. Unit of electrical energy for billing

questions.question: RCD (Residual Current Device) trips when
options: current in live ≠ current in neutral (earth leakage detected)
questions.answer: current in live ≠ current in neutral (earth leakage detected)
questions.explanation: RCD detects tiny current imbalances (as small as 30mA) suggesting earth fault → cuts power in milliseconds

questions.question: 100W bulb for 5 hours uses how many kWh?
questions.explanation: Energy=0.1kW×5h=0.5kWh

questions.question: Double insulation (class II) appliances
questions.explanation: Class II: plastic casing, two layers of insulation. No conducting parts exposed → no earth needed

questions.question: Neutral wire is at approximately
questions.explanation: Neutral is return path connected to earth at substation — approximately 0V (potential). Live swings ±240V

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Faraday's Law:
EMF = -N × ΔΦ/Δt
Φ = BA cosθ (magnetic flux)
N = number of turns

Lenz's Law: induced current opposes change causing it

AC Generator:
EMF = NBAω sin(ωt) = E₀ sin(ωt)
E₀ = NBAω (peak EMF)
ω = 2πf (angular frequency)

Transformer:
Vₛ/Vₚ = Nₛ/Nₚ = Iₚ/Iₛ (ideal)

content.title: Transformer
content.body:
Step-up transformer: Nₛ>Nₚ → Vₛ>Vₚ
Nₚ=100, Nₛ=1000, Vₚ=240V
Vₛ=240×1000/100=2400V
If Iₛ=0.1A → Iₚ=0.1×1000/100=1A
Power in = Power out (ideal): VₚIₚ=VₛIₛ=240W

questions.question: Faraday's law: EMF depends on
options: rate of change of magnetic flux
questions.answer: rate of change of magnetic flux
questions.explanation: EMF = -NdΦ/dt: EMF proportional to rate of flux change

questions.question: Lenz's law states induced current
options: opposes the change in flux that caused it
questions.answer: opposes the change in flux that caused it
questions.explanation: Lenz's law: energy conservation — induced current creates field opposing original change

questions.question: AC generator produces sinusoidal EMF because
options: the coil rotates, changing flux sinusoidally
questions.answer: the coil rotates, changing flux sinusoidally
questions.explanation: Rotating coil: flux = BAcosθ = BAcos(ωt), EMF = −dΦ/dt = NBAω sin(ωt)

questions.question: Peak EMF of generator N=100,B=0.1T,A=0.02m²,f=50Hz
RMS voltage relates to peak by
questions.explanation: RMS (root mean square) = peak/√2 for sinusoidal AC

questions.question: Step-up transformer increases
questions.explanation: Step-up: Nₛ>Nₚ → Vₛ>Vₚ. Power conserved (ideal): higher V, lower I

questions.question: Transformer ratio: Nₚ=500, Nₛ=50, Vₚ=220V. Vₛ=
Electricity transmitted at high voltage because
options: I²R losses reduced with lower current (P=IV constant)
questions.answer: I²R losses reduced with lower current (P=IV constant)
questions.explanation: High V → low I for same power → power loss P=I²R much smaller → efficient long-distance transmission

questions.question: Ideal transformer: Vₛ=100V, Iₛ=2A, Vₚ=20V. Primary current Iₚ=
questions.explanation: Power conserved: VₚIₚ=VₛIₛ → 20×Iₚ=100×2=200 → Iₚ=10A

questions.question: Mutual induction occurs when
options: changing current in one coil induces EMF in nearby coil
questions.answer: changing current in one coil induces EMF in nearby coil
questions.explanation: Mutual induction: basis of transformers. Changing flux from primary coil links secondary

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?

content.body:
Cathode ray tube (CRT):
Electrons emitted from heated cathode
Accelerated by anode voltage V
Kinetic energy: ½mv² = eV
Speed: v = √(2eV/m)
e/m ratio (Thomson) ≈ 1.76×10¹¹ C/kg

Millikan oil drop: measured e = 1.6×10⁻¹⁹ C
∴ m_electron = 9.11×10⁻³¹ kg

content.title: CRT Applications
content.body:
Oscilloscope: displays voltage-time graphs
X-plates: horizontal deflection (time base)
Y-plates: vertical deflection (signal)
Electron gun: produces focused electron beam

TV/monitor (old): scanning electron beam across screen
Modern: LCD/LED screens replaced CRT

content.title: Photoelectric Effect
content.body:
Light ejects electrons from metal surface
Einstein: photons with energy E=hf
Threshold frequency f₀: minimum to eject electron
hf = φ + ½mv²max
φ = work function (energy to release electron)
h = 6.63×10⁻³⁴ J⋅s (Planck's constant)

questions.question: Cathode rays are
questions.explanation: Cathode rays are streams of electrons emitted from heated cathode in evacuated tube

questions.question: Electron kinetic energy after acceleration through voltage V
questions.explanation: Work done by electric field: W=qV=eV = kinetic energy gained

questions.question: Speed of electron accelerated through 1000V (e=1.6×10⁻¹⁹, m=9.11×10⁻³¹)
Millikan's oil drop experiment measured
questions.explanation: Millikan balanced gravity and electric force on charged oil drops to determine e

questions.question: In oscilloscope, Y-plates deflect electron beam
questions.explanation: Y-plates: vertical deflection proportional to input voltage being measured

questions.question: Photoelectric effect shows light behaves as
questions.explanation: Photoelectric effect: light comes in discrete packets (photons E=hf). Proves particle nature of light

questions.question: Threshold frequency in photoelectric effect is
options: minimum frequency to eject electrons from metal
questions.answer: minimum frequency to eject electrons from metal
questions.explanation: Below threshold frequency, no electrons ejected regardless of intensity — needs minimum energy per photon

questions.question: Einstein's photoelectric equation: hf = φ + ½mv²max means
options: photon energy = work function + maximum KE of ejected electron
questions.answer: photon energy = work function + maximum KE of ejected electron
questions.explanation: Photon energy (hf) used to overcome work function (φ) with remainder as kinetic energy of electron

questions.question: Increasing light INTENSITY in photoelectric effect (above threshold)
options: increases number of electrons emitted per second
questions.answer: increases number of electrons emitted per second
questions.explanation: More photons per second → more electrons per second. Max KE depends only on frequency, not intensity

questions.question: Planck's constant h has value
questions.explanation: h=6.63×10⁻³⁴ J⋅s — relates photon energy to frequency: E=hf

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Electricity Detail. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Electricity Detail concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Electricity Detail builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Electricity Detail. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Electricity Detail. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Electricity Detail include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Electricity Detail. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
